title: Stuck in the Office: Why Millions of Americans Cannot Quit Their Jobs
subtitle: A new report highlights how rising healthcare costs are forcing workers to stay in positions they dislike.

summary.en: A recent survey shows that one in four American workers stays in a job they dislike to keep their health insurance. This phenomenon, known as 'job lock,' has grown significantly over the last five years. Experts warn that this trend hurts both individual workers and the broader economy. Many employees fear that leaving their current employer would make healthcare unaffordable. Policy experts suggest that current insurance systems need reform to allow for more job mobility.
summary.zh: 近期一項調查顯示，四分之一的美國勞工為了保留醫療保險而留在不喜歡的工作崗位上。這種被稱為「職位鎖定」的現象在過去五年中顯著增加。專家警告，此趨勢對個別勞工及整體經濟皆有害。許多員工擔心離開現職將導致醫療費用難以負擔。政策專家建議，現行的保險制度需要改革，以促進更高的就業流動性。

paragraphs.en: For many Americans, the decision to quit a job is no longer just about finding a better salary or a new career path. A new report from the West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America reveals that nearly one in four workers who receive health insurance through their employer are staying in jobs they do not want. This situation is often called "job lock," and it has become a growing problem for the American workforce.
paragraphs.zh: 對許多美國人而言，辭職的決定不再只是為了尋求更好的薪資或新的職涯發展。一份來自「西健康蓋洛普美國醫療中心 (West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America)」的最新報告顯示，近四分之一透過雇主獲得醫療保險的勞工，正困在他們不想做的職位上。這種情況通常被稱為「職位鎖定」，已成為美國勞動力日益嚴重的問題。

paragraphs.en: According to the West Health-Gallup report, approximately 23 million adults are currently experiencing job lock. This is a significant increase from 2021, when only 16% of workers reported being in this position. Ellyn Maese, a research director for the center, described the trend as very serious. She noted that it is concerning to see so many people feeling forced to stay in roles they want to leave simply to keep their medical coverage.
paragraphs.zh: 根據西健康蓋洛普的報告，目前約有兩千三百萬名成年人正經歷職位鎖定。這與二〇二一年相比有顯著增加，當時僅有百分之十六的勞工表示處於這種處境。該中心的研究總監艾琳·梅斯 (Ellyn Maese) 將此趨勢形容為非常嚴重。她指出，看到這麼多人為了維持醫療保障，而被迫留在想離開的職位上，令人感到擔憂。

paragraphs.en: This trend is even more common among people with serious health issues. The report found that 41% of working adults who have three or more chronic health conditions—long-term illnesses that require ongoing medical care—are staying in their jobs specifically for the health insurance benefits. For these individuals, leaving a job could mean losing access to the treatments they rely on to stay healthy.
paragraphs.zh: 這種趨勢在患有嚴重健康問題的人群中更為普遍。報告發現，在患有三種或以上慢性病（需要長期醫療照護的長期疾病）的在職成年人中，有百分之四十一的人是為了醫療保險福利而留在原職。對這些人來說，離職可能意味著失去維持健康所需的治療管道。

paragraphs.en: Experts suggest that this issue is closely tied to the rising cost of medical care in the United States. Larry Levitt, an executive vice president at the health research organization KFF, explained that healthcare is currently at the top of the list for economic worries among Americans. An April poll from KFF found that nearly two-thirds of adults are anxious about being able to afford healthcare, placing it on the same level of concern as the cost of gas and transportation. Because of this, it is logical that workers would be afraid to leave an unwanted job if it means losing their insurance.
paragraphs.zh: 專家認為，這個問題與美國醫療費用不斷上漲密切相關。醫療研究機構 KFF 的執行副總裁賴瑞·李維特 (Larry Levitt) 解釋，醫療保健目前是美國人經濟擔憂清單上的首位。KFF 四月份的一項民調發現，近三分之二的成年人擔心自己負擔不起醫療費用，這與對油價及交通費用的擔憂程度相當。因此，勞工若因擔心失去保險而不敢離開不喜歡的工作，在邏輯上是可以理解的。

paragraphs.en: Beyond the personal stress, there are concerns about how this affects the U.S. economy. Maese explained that unhappy employees are often less effective in their work. Furthermore, a healthy economy usually relies on people being able to move between jobs, start new businesses, or explore different career opportunities. When people feel trapped in their current roles, they are less likely to take the risks necessary to help the economy grow. This situation is made worse by a pessimistic job market; a May Gallup poll found that only 28% of workers believe it is a good time to find a new job, which is the lowest level recorded since 2013.
paragraphs.zh: 除了個人壓力外，人們也擔心這對美國經濟造成的影響。梅斯解釋，不快樂的員工通常工作效率較低。此外，健康的經濟通常仰賴人們能夠在不同工作間轉換、創業或探索不同的職涯機會。當人們感到被困在目前的職位時，他們較不可能承擔促進經濟成長所需的風險。悲觀的就業市場加劇了這種情況；蓋洛普五月份的民調發現，僅有百分之二十八的勞工認為現在是找新工作的好時機，這是自二〇一三年以來的最低紀錄。

paragraphs.en: Some experts point to changes in government policy as a reason for this shift. In 2021, the U.S. Congress increased financial help for middle-income people to buy their own insurance through the Affordable Care Act (ACA) marketplaces. The ACA is a law designed to make health insurance more available and affordable for more people. However, when Congress allowed these extra subsidies—financial support provided by the government—to end in 2025, many people lost that help. Levitt noted that the end of these subsidies has made it harder for people to afford insurance on their own, effectively pushing them back into jobs they might otherwise have quit.
paragraphs.zh: 一些專家將政府政策的變動視為造成這種轉變的原因。二〇二一年，美國國會增加了對中等收入者的財務補助，協助他們透過「平價醫療法案 (ACA)」市場購買自己的保險。平價醫療法案是一項旨在讓更多人能獲得且負擔得起醫療保險的法律。然而，當國會允許這些額外的補貼（由政府提供的財務支持）於二〇二五年結束時，許多人失去了這項協助。李維特指出，這些補貼的終止使得人們更難自行負擔保險，實際上將他們推回了原本可能已經辭職的工作崗位上。

paragraphs.en: Michael Cannon, the director of health policy studies at the Cato Institute, agrees that job lock is a real and significant issue. He argues that the current system of linking health insurance to employment is flawed. Cannon suggests that the country needs reform to move toward a system where insurance belongs to the individual rather than the employer. He believes that if people could purchase their own insurance that stays with them even when they change jobs, it would increase income mobility and reduce the gaps in coverage that many workers currently face.
paragraphs.zh: 卡托研究所 (Cato Institute) 的健康政策研究主任麥可·坎農 (Michael Cannon) 同意職位鎖定是一個真實且重大的問題。他主張，目前將醫療保險與就業掛鉤的制度是有缺陷的。坎農建議，國家需要進行改革，朝向保險屬於個人而非雇主的制度邁進。他認為，如果人們能夠購買屬於自己的保險，即使在更換工作時也能持續享有，這將能提高收入流動性，並減少許多勞工目前面臨的保障缺口。

paragraphs.en: As the number of people in job lock continues to rise, the debate over how to fix the system is likely to grow louder. For now, millions of workers remain in positions they dislike, balancing their desire for a better career against the fear of losing their access to essential medical care. Whether through policy changes or shifts in the job market, the pressure on the American workforce remains a central challenge for the country's economic and health systems.
paragraphs.zh: 隨著陷入職位鎖定的人數持續增加，關於如何修復該制度的辯論可能會愈演愈烈。目前，數百萬名勞工仍留在他們不喜歡的職位上，在追求更好職涯的渴望與失去基本醫療照護管道的恐懼之間掙扎。無論是透過政策變革還是就業市場的轉變，美國勞動力所承受的壓力，仍是該國經濟與醫療體系面臨的核心挑戰。

keyTerms.definitionEn: A situation where someone is unable to leave their current job because they depend on the benefits it provides.
keyTerms.definitionZh: 鎖定；指因依賴福利而無法離職的狀態。
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作名詞（鎖），這裡指一種受限的狀態。文中：This situation is often called "job lock," and it has become a growing problem for the American workforce.

keyTerms.definitionEn: Connected to or caused by something else.
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作動詞（綁），這裡指因果關係。文中：Experts suggest that this issue is closely tied to the rising cost of medical care in the United States.

keyTerms.definitionEn: Forcing or pressuring someone to do something they might not want to do.
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作物理上的「推」，這裡指心理或環境上的強迫。文中：Levitt noted that the end of these subsidies has made it harder for people to afford insurance on their own, effectively pushing them back into jobs they might otherwise have quit.

sources.name: NPR Business
sources.title: Staying in a job for the health insurance? About 1 in 4 Americans do, a survey says

previousArticles.title: Why Millions of Americans Stay in Jobs They Dislike
previousArticles.summaryEn: A new survey shows that nearly one in four American workers stays in a job they do not like just to keep their health insurance. This situation, often called 'job lock,' has become more common over the last five years. Experts say that high healthcare costs make it difficult for people to leave their positions. When workers feel stuck, they are less productive and less likely to start new businesses. Many analysts believe that changing how insurance is provided could help the economy grow.

quiz.question: According to the report, what is the specific percentage of workers with three or more chronic conditions who remain in their jobs primarily for health insurance?
quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 D。根據文章第三段明確指出，患有三種或以上慢性病的勞工中，有 41% 是為了健保福利而留在職位上。選項 A 是 2021 年報告的整體勞工比例，選項 B 是目前受「工作鎖定」影響的總人數（2300 萬），選項 C 則是認為目前是找工作好時機的勞工比例，皆非題目所問之數據。

quiz.question: Based on the information provided, why might the end of government subsidies in 2025 have contributed to the increase in 'job lock'?

options.A: It forced employers to stop providing health insurance to their staff.
options.B: It made individual health insurance plans significantly more expensive for workers.
options.C: It caused the job market to become more competitive for middle-income workers.
options.D: It encouraged more people to start their own businesses instead of staying employed.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 B。文章第六段提到，政府補助的終止使得人們自行負擔保險變得更困難，因此被迫回到雇主提供的保險體系中。選項 A 與文意相反，補助結束影響的是個人市場而非雇主義務；選項 C 並未在文中提及；選項 D 則與「工作鎖定」導致人們不敢離職創業的現象完全矛盾。

quiz.question: In the fifth paragraph, what does the word 'pessimistic' mean as it is used to describe the job market?

options.B: Lacking hope or confidence in positive outcomes.
options.D: Focused primarily on long-term career growth.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 B。文中提到「pessimistic job market」並隨後引用數據說明僅有 28% 的勞工認為現在是找工作的好時機，這顯示勞工對就業前景感到悲觀且缺乏信心。選項 A 描述的是競爭激烈，與悲觀無直接關聯；選項 C 與 D 雖為職場相關概念，但並非「pessimistic」一詞在該語境下的定義。

quiz.question: Which of the following best summarizes the central message of the article?

options.A: The U.S. government should increase taxes to fund better healthcare for all citizens.
options.B: The rising cost of medical care is the primary reason why companies are failing.
options.C: Linking health insurance to employment creates a 'job lock' that negatively impacts both workers and the economy.
options.D: Most Americans prefer to stay in their current jobs rather than explore new career opportunities.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。本文的核心在於探討「工作鎖定」（job lock）現象，即醫療保險與雇主掛鉤導致勞工不敢離職，進而影響個人職涯發展與整體經濟活力。選項 A 超出文章討論範圍；選項 B 將焦點錯誤地放在公司經營而非勞工選擇；選項 D 忽略了文章強調的「被迫」因素，多數人其實是因恐懼失去保險才留任，而非主動選擇。

aiDisclaimer: 本文由 AI 綜合多家報導生成，事實請以原始來源為準。
